does: This paper introduces EDGE-EVAL, an industry-oriented benchmarking framework that evaluates large language models beyond traditional accuracy metrics to assess their viability for real-world deployment. The framework evaluates LLaMA and Qwen model variants on legacy NVIDIA Tesla T4 GPUs across three industrial tasks, introducing five novel deployment metrics: Economic Break-Even (measuring profitability thresholds), Intelligence-Per-Watt (energy efficiency), System Density (hardware scaling capabilities), Cold-Start Tax (serverless deployment overhead), and Quantization Fidelity (compression safety). By measuring these operational and economic criteria alongside traditional performance metrics, the work empirically demonstrates which model configurations are economically viable for actual industry deployment under strict constraints on energy consumption, latency, and hardware costs.
connects: This work responds to the deployment-evaluation gap identified in recent LLM research, where standard benchmarks prioritize accuracy while ignoring the operational realities of production systems. It builds on growing recognition that efficiency and cost matter critically for industrial AI, extending prior work on model compression, quantization, and energy-aware evaluation. The paper connects to earlier research on inference optimization for resource-constrained environments and to economic analyses of machine learning systems. It also engages with practical deployment challenges documented in healthcare AI, financial systems, and enterprise applications, where real-world constraints on hardware, energy budgets, and latency requirements diverge sharply from academic evaluation paradigms.
enables: This framework enables more realistic assessment of LLM viability, potentially shifting industry adoption decisions away from largest models toward smaller, efficient alternatives when economic constraints are primary. By introducing quantified deployment metrics, the work facilitates systematic comparison of models under realistic operational scenarios, supporting decisions about whether to deploy on-premises, adopt serverless architectures, or implement model compression. The benchmarking approach could inspire similar industry-focused evaluation frameworks across other domains and encourage research toward models explicitly optimized for economic efficiency rather than benchmark scores. These metrics may also guide future model development toward variants that satisfy strict deployment constraints while maintaining sufficient capability.
